section.title: The Coming of the Lord
section.sectionRationale: The church grieved for believers who had died, fearing they would miss the Lord's return. Paul answers with the surest comfort in the New Testament: because Jesus died and rose, God will raise the dead in Christ, and living and dead together will be caught up to meet the Lord and be with Him forever.
section.thesis: Christians do not grieve like those without hope, because the death and resurrection of Jesus guarantee that God will raise the dead in Christ, that the living will not precede them, and that both together will be caught up to be forever with the Lord.
section.frameNote: Two movements: grief transformed by the hope of resurrection grounded in Jesus who died and rose; and the order of events at the Lord's coming, ending with the comfort that we will always be with the Lord.

verses.text: Brothers, we do not want you to be uninformed about those who {{koimaomai|sleep}} in death, so that you will not grieve like the rest, who are without {{elpis|hope}}.
For since we believe that Jesus died and rose again, we also believe that God will bring with Jesus those who have fallen asleep in Him.

units.label: The order at the Lord's coming
units.note: the dead raised, the living caught up, all with the Lord

verses.text: By the word of the Lord, we declare to you that we who are alive and remain until the {{parousia|coming}} of the Lord will by no means precede those who have fallen asleep.

For the Lord Himself will descend from heaven with a loud {{keleusma|command}}, with the voice of an {{archangelos|archangel}}, and with the {{salpinx|trumpet}} of God, and the dead in Christ will be the first to rise.
After that, we who are alive and remain will be {{harpazo|caught up}} together with them in the clouds to {{apantesis|meet}} the Lord in the air. And so we will always be with the Lord.
Therefore {{parakaleo|encourage}} one another with these words.

koimaomai.gloss: To fall asleep, used gently for the death of believers. Paul repeatedly speaks of those who sleep in death, a tender image that assumes an awakening. Death for the Christian is not annihilation but a sleep from which God will raise them, so the word itself already carries the hope of resurrection.

elpis.gloss: Hope, confident expectation grounded in God's promise. Paul says the world grieves as those without hope, but believers do not. Christian hope is not vague optimism but sure confidence in resurrection, anchored in the fact that Jesus rose. This hope reshapes even how believers mourn.

parousia.gloss: Coming, arrival, presence, used for the return of the Lord Jesus. The whole passage centers on the parousia, when the Lord descends and gathers His people. It is the great future event toward which Christian hope strains, the moment when the dead are raised and the living are caught up to be with Him.

keleusma.gloss: A loud command or shout of authority, like an officer's cry that sets an army in motion. The Lord descends with a keleusma, a commanding shout that summons the dead to rise. The word conveys the sovereign authority of Christ's return, a royal command that creation and the grave must obey.

archangelos.gloss: An archangel, a chief or ruling angel. The Lord's coming is accompanied by the voice of an archangel, part of the majestic, public, unmistakable character of His return. Far from a secret event, Christ's coming is heralded with heavenly authority and splendor.

salpinx.gloss: A trumpet. The trumpet of God sounds at the Lord's coming, echoing the Old Testament use of trumpets to announce God's presence, assembly, and decisive acts. It signals the gathering of God's people and the climactic moment of resurrection, a summons no one can miss.

harpazo.gloss: To seize, snatch, or catch up suddenly and forcibly. Believers who are alive will be caught up together with the risen dead to meet the Lord. The Latin translation of this word, rapere, gives us the term rapture. It pictures God powerfully and swiftly gathering His people to Himself at Christ's coming.

apantesis.gloss: A meeting, especially a formal going out to welcome an arriving dignitary and escort him in. Believers go up to meet the Lord in the air, a picture drawn from cities greeting a visiting king. The word suggests not a departure to somewhere else but a joyful welcome of the returning Lord.

parakaleo.gloss: To encourage, comfort, or exhort, to come alongside with strengthening words. Paul ends by telling them to encourage one another with these words. The teaching about the Lord's coming is given not to satisfy curiosity but to comfort grieving hearts, and it is meant to be shared for mutual strengthening.

basic.context: Some believers in Thessalonica had died, and the church was distressed, apparently fearing that those who died would miss out when the Lord returned. Paul writes to comfort them. His answer is simple and glorious: Jesus died and rose, so those who die in Him will be raised, and everyone, living and dead, will be together with the Lord forever.
basic.hermeneutics: This is teaching given for comfort, not a puzzle to be decoded for a timeline. Paul wants grieving Christians to be reassured, so he tells them what will surely happen at the Lord's coming. Christians disagree on some details of the end times, but the central promise, resurrection and being with the Lord forever, is clear and certain.
basic.originalLanguages: Paul calls death a koimaomai, a falling asleep, so believers do not grieve like those without elpis, hope. At the parousia, the Lord's coming, He descends with a keleusma, a commanding shout, the voice of an archangelos, archangel, and the salpinx, trumpet, of God. The living are harpazo, caught up, to apantesis, meet, the Lord. So Paul says, parakaleo, encourage one another.
basic.typology: The trumpet and the descent of the Lord echo God coming down at Sinai with trumpet blast, and the gathering of God's scattered people promised by the prophets. Going out to meet the arriving Lord pictures a city welcoming its king, now fulfilled in Christ's return.
basic.christ: Everything rests on Jesus who died and rose. Because He was raised, those who sleep in Him will be raised too. He Himself descends from heaven, gathers His people, and brings them to be with Him forever. The Christian's whole hope in death is the risen and returning Christ.
basic.god: God is the one who raised Jesus and who will bring the dead in Christ with Him. He acts to reunite His people and to keep His promises even beyond the grave. The God who conquered death in Jesus will do the same for all who belong to Him.
basic.crossReferences: First Corinthians 15 is Paul's fullest teaching on the resurrection and the last trumpet. John 11 records Jesus as the resurrection and the life. And Second Thessalonians 2 again speaks of the coming of the Lord and our being gathered to Him.
basic.oneStory: Death entered the world through sin, but God promised to defeat it. In the risen Jesus that victory is won, and here Paul unfolds what it means for those who die in Christ: they will rise, and with the living they will be caught up to be with the Lord forever, the sure end of the whole biblical story.

context.literary: This is the first of two closely linked passages on the last things, answering a question about believers who have died, before chapter five turns to the timing of the day of the Lord. The structure moves from the pastoral principle, do not grieve without hope, to its ground in Jesus death and resurrection, to a detailed word of the Lord about the sequence of events, and back to the pastoral aim, encourage one another. The frame is comfort, not speculation.
context.historical: The Thessalonians had received Paul's teaching about Christ's return but were unsettled when members of the congregation died before it happened. Their grief suggests confusion about whether the dead would share in the coming glory. Paul, writing soon after founding the church, addresses this fresh pastoral crisis with a specific reassurance grounded in apostolic teaching he calls the word of the Lord.
context.canonical: Together with 1 Corinthians 15, this is one of the two central New Testament passages on the resurrection of believers and the return of Christ. Verse 17 is the source of the term rapture, from the Latin for caught up, and the passage has been much discussed in debates over the end times, though its primary and clear purpose is to comfort the grieving with resurrection hope.

hermeneutics.authorIntent: To comfort a grieving church by assuring them that believers who have died will lose nothing at the Lord's coming but will be raised first, and that all God's people, living and dead, will be caught up together to be forever with the Lord.
hermeneutics.descriptionVsPrescription: The passage mainly describes what will happen at Christ's coming, given as a sure word of the Lord. Its one command is pastoral: encourage one another with these words. The certain hope of resurrection and reunion with the Lord is meant to be believed and to shape how Christians face death and comfort one another.

debates.question: How does the catching up, or rapture, relate to Christ's return?

views.label: One glorious return
views.case: Most read this as a single, public, glorious coming of the Lord at which the dead are raised and the living are caught up to welcome and escort Him, as a city goes out to meet its king.

views.label: A distinct rapture event
views.case: Some traditions distinguish a catching up of the church from a later visible return; they read verse 17 as describing believers being taken before a period of tribulation.

debates.bearing: The debate concerns sequence and timing; the shared and central certainty is resurrection and being forever with the Lord, which is the passage's comforting point.

debates.question: What is the state of those who sleep in death?

views.label: Present with the Lord, awaiting resurrection
views.case: Sleep describes the body; the believer is with Christ at death, and the body will be raised at His coming, so nothing of the person is lost.

views.label: A gentle image for death itself
views.case: Sleep chiefly softens the language of death and assumes an awakening; the stress falls on the certain resurrection rather than on the intermediate state.

debates.bearing: Either way, death for the believer is not the end; God will raise them, and they will be with the Lord forever.

study.originalLanguages: Paul speaks of believers who koimaomai, sleep, in death, so that Christians need not grieve like those with no elpis, hope. The ground is the historical fact that Jesus died and rose, on the basis of which God will bring the sleeping ones with Him at the parousia, the coming of the Lord. That coming is majestic and unmistakable: the Lord descends with a keleusma, a commanding shout, the voice of an archangelos, archangel, and the salpinx, the trumpet of God, at which the dead in Christ rise first. Then the living are harpazo, snatched up, to apantesis, a formal welcome, of the Lord in the air, so that they are forever with Him. The passage ends with the imperative to parakaleo, to comfort and encourage one another with these very words.

typology.type: The trumpet and descent of the Lord
typology.fulfillment: Echoes God descending on Sinai with trumpet blast, now fulfilled in Christ descending from heaven to gather His people.

typology.type: Going out to meet the arriving king
typology.fulfillment: The custom of a city welcoming a visiting dignitary is fulfilled as believers rise to meet and escort the returning Lord Jesus.

study.christ: The entire hope of the passage stands on Jesus who died and rose again. Because He was raised, God will raise those who have fallen asleep in Him. Christ Himself is the actor at the climax: the Lord descends from heaven with a commanding shout, raises the dead in Him, and gathers the living to Himself. The goal is not merely resurrection but union: we will always be with the Lord. In death and beyond it, the believer's comfort is the risen, returning Christ who will not lose one of His own.
study.god: God is the one who raised Jesus from the dead and who will, on that same basis, bring with Jesus those who have died in Him. The whole event is God keeping His promise to defeat death and to gather His people. He is a God who reunites, who acts in power at the last trumpet, and who guarantees that nothing, not even the grave, can separate His people from Him. The comfort of grieving believers rests on the character and power of this God.

crossReferences.note: Christ the firstfruits guarantees the resurrection of those who belong to Him at His coming.
At the last trumpet the dead are raised and the living changed, closely paralleling this passage.
Jesus declares He is the resurrection and the life, the ground of the hope Paul teaches here.
Paul again speaks of the coming of the Lord Jesus and our being gathered together to Him.

study.oneStory: Death entered the world through sin, but from the first promise in Eden God pledged to undo it. In the death and resurrection of Jesus that victory is decisively won, and here Paul unfolds what it means for those who die in Christ. They will not be left behind or forgotten; they will rise first, and with the living they will be caught up to meet the Lord and be with Him forever. This is where the whole biblical story is heading: the return of the King, the raising of the dead, and an unending life in the presence of God.

sermon.title: What to Say to the Grieving: 1 Thessalonians 4:13-18, Part 1
sermon.why: Piper shows how the resurrection hope does not erase grief but gives it ballast, so believers grieve differently from those without hope, and he grounds that comfort in Jesus death and resurrection, the heart of this movement.

quiz.q: Why does Paul say Christians do not grieve like the rest of the world?

options.text: Because they have hope, grounded in Jesus death and resurrection.
options.note: Verses 13-14 base this hope on Jesus dying and rising.

options.text: Because Christians are not supposed to feel sadness at all.
options.note: Paul does not forbid grief; he transforms it with hope.

options.text: Because death is not real for believers.
options.note: Death is real, but it is a sleep from which God will raise them.

options.text: Because they will avoid dying altogether.
options.note: Believers do die, yet they will be raised; that is the hope.

quiz.q: What happens first at the coming of the Lord, according to verse 16?

options.text: The dead in Christ rise first.
options.note: Verse 16 says the dead in Christ will be the first to rise.

options.text: The living are taken and the dead are left behind.
options.note: The reverse: the dead rise first, then the living are caught up with them.

options.text: The world is destroyed before anyone rises.
options.note: The focus is the Lord descending and the dead rising, not prior destruction.

options.text: Nothing happens to those who have died.
options.note: The dead in Christ are precisely the ones raised first.

quiz.q: What is the final comfort Paul gives about the Lord's coming?

options.text: We will always be with the Lord.
options.note: Verse 17 ends with the promise of being forever with the Lord.

options.text: We will finally learn the exact date of the end.
options.note: The comfort is being with the Lord, not knowing a date.

options.text: We will escape all future responsibility.
options.note: The hope is eternal union with the Lord, not escape.

options.text: We will be separated from other believers.
options.note: The living and the dead are caught up together, united with the Lord.

quiz.q: On what does Paul base the certainty that the dead in Christ will be raised?

options.text: On the fact that Jesus Himself died and rose again.
options.note: Verse 14 grounds the resurrection of believers in Jesus own resurrection.

options.text: On human optimism about the future.
options.note: The ground is Jesus resurrection, not optimism.

options.text: On the good behavior of the believers.
options.note: The basis is Christ's work, not the believers merits.

options.text: On a guess about what might happen.
options.note: It is a sure word of the Lord, grounded in Jesus resurrection.

quiz.q: For what purpose does Paul give this teaching about the Lord's coming?

options.text: So that believers would encourage one another with these words.
options.note: Verse 18 states the purpose: mutual encouragement.

options.text: So that they could calculate the date of Christ's return.
options.note: The aim is comfort, not date-setting.

options.text: So that they would argue about end-times charts.
options.note: The purpose is encouragement, not controversy.

options.text: So that they would fear the future.
options.note: The teaching gives comfort and hope, not fear.

questions: Paul says Christians grieve, but not as those without hope. How does the promise of resurrection change the way you face the death of those you love?
The whole hope of this passage rests on Jesus having died and risen. How settled is your confidence in the resurrection of Jesus, and how does it steady you?
The dead in Christ lose nothing at His coming; they rise first. How does that reassure you about believers who have already died?
The goal of it all is that we will always be with the Lord. Does being with Jesus forever feel like your deepest hope, and how might it become more so?
Paul says to encourage one another with these words. Who in your life is grieving or afraid, and how could you comfort them with this hope this week?

translationNotes.note: The BSB footnote notes that those who sleep in death renders a phrase literally meaning those who have fallen asleep, the same expression used in verses 14 and 15.
Caught up translates harpazo; the Latin rendering rapere is the source of the English term rapture.
To meet the Lord renders apantesis, a word used for a delegation going out to welcome and escort an arriving dignitary.

meta.notes: BSB text fetched from bible.helloao.org for 1 Thessalonians 4:13-18. No em dashes required adjustment in this passage.
